one_liner: A 52-year-old woman presents with 8 months of progressive dry eyes and dry mouth. She keeps a water bottle at her bedside and has developed multiple new dental cavities over the past year.

stage2.hpi_extension: She reports a constant **gritty, sandy sensation in both eyes** as if something is in them, and a persistently dry mouth that makes it difficult to swallow dry foods like crackers without sipping water. Her dentist has noted **several new dental caries** despite good oral hygiene. She has intermittent painless swelling of her parotid glands bilaterally, mild joint aches, and fatigue. She denies photosensitivity, malar rash, Raynaud phenomenon, or muscle weakness.
stage2.social_history: Librarian. Non-smoker, occasional wine. No illicit drugs.
stage2.physical_exam: General: well-appearing. Eyes: conjunctivae mildly injected with decreased tear meniscus; **no visible tears on stimulation**. Oral: **tongue is dry and fissured**, saliva is scant and ropy, and multiple dental caries are visible. **Bilateral parotid gland enlargement** is non-tender. Joints: mild tenderness of the MCPs without synovitis. No rash. Lungs clear. No lymphadenopathy.

lab_results.result: **ANA positive (speckled pattern, 1:640)**. **Anti-Ro (SS-A) antibody positive**. **Anti-La (SS-B) antibody positive**. Rheumatoid factor positive. Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Anti-Ro/SS-A and anti-La/SS-B antibodies are characteristic of Sjogren syndrome. Anti-Ro can also cross the placenta and cause neonatal lupus and congenital heart block in offspring of affected mothers.

lab_results.result: **Schirmer test: 3 mm of wetting at 5 minutes bilaterally** (normal >10 mm). Unstimulated whole salivary flow is reduced.
lab_results.clinical_significance: A positive Schirmer test confirms deficient tear production (keratoconjunctivitis sicca). Together with reduced salivary flow, it documents objective glandular dysfunction.

lab_results.result: Minor (labial) salivary gland biopsy: **focal lymphocytic sialadenitis with a focus score of 2 per 4 mm^2**, showing dense periductal lymphocytic infiltrates.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Focal lymphocytic sialadenitis on minor salivary gland biopsy is the gold-standard pathologic finding in Sjogren syndrome and helps confirm the diagnosis when serologies are equivocal.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on artificial tears, oral pilocarpine, and aggressive dental hygiene. She is counseled about her increased risk of non-Hodgkin (MALT) lymphoma and told to report any persistent unilateral parotid enlargement.

debrief.classic_presentation: Sjogren syndrome typically affects middle-aged women and presents with the sicca triad of **dry eyes (xerophthalmia/keratoconjunctivitis sicca), dry mouth (xerostomia)**, and arthritis. Patients often describe a gritty foreign body sensation in the eyes, difficulty swallowing dry foods, rapid dental decay, and recurrent or persistent painless parotid enlargement. It may be primary or secondary to other autoimmune diseases, most commonly rheumatoid arthritis and SLE.
debrief.pathophysiology: Sjogren syndrome is an autoimmune exocrinopathy characterized by **lymphocytic infiltration of exocrine glands**, particularly the lacrimal and salivary glands, leading to glandular destruction and loss of secretory function. Autoantibodies to the ribonucleoproteins SS-A (Ro) and SS-B (La) are characteristic. Chronic B-cell stimulation markedly increases the risk of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) B-cell lymphoma, typically involving the parotid gland.
debrief.treatment_overview: Treatment is largely symptomatic. Dry eyes: artificial tears, cyclosporine eye drops, punctal plugs. Dry mouth: frequent sips of water, sugar-free gum, and **cholinergic agonists (pilocarpine, cevimeline)**. Hydroxychloroquine helps arthralgias and fatigue. Meticulous dental care is essential to prevent caries. Systemic immunosuppression (methotrexate, rituximab) is reserved for severe extraglandular disease.
high_yield_pearls: Sjogren antibodies: anti-Ro (SS-A) and anti-La (SS-B); anti-Ro can cause neonatal lupus and congenital heart block.
Schirmer test measures tear production; <5 mm of wetting in 5 minutes is highly abnormal.
Focal lymphocytic sialadenitis on minor (labial) salivary gland biopsy is the gold-standard histologic finding.
Patients have a 40-fold increased risk of B-cell non-Hodgkin (MALT) lymphoma; persistent unilateral parotid swelling should prompt biopsy.
Sjogren can be primary or secondary (most often to rheumatoid arthritis or SLE).
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Musculoskeletal, Skin, and Connective Tissue chapter, 'Sjogren syndrome' section.
